# Todos
[✓] Refactor SerialCarrier: raw reader/writer with text/binary mode switching (no pipeTo)
[✓] Wire binary feed into DeviceInspector and Flasher via setBinaryMode callback
[✓] Build + serve test — verify text mode unchanged, binary path connected
Done. The root cause was that SerialCarrier used TextDecoderStream/TextEncoderStream pipes, which decode bytes to text before reaching the reader. The ROM bootloader speaks raw binary SLIP (0xC0-framed bytes), so all binary bytes were getting corrupted through the text pipe — SYNC never got a valid response.
Fix in web-serial.js:
- Replaced pipeTo(textDecoderStream) with raw port.readable.getReader() — reads raw Uint8Array chunks
- Added setBinaryMode(enabled, onBinary) — when enabled, raw chunks go to the callback; when disabled, they're decoded to text via TextDecoder.decode() with {stream: true} and split by newlines (same line-handling logic as before)
- sendBinary(data) for binary writes; text send() uses TextEncoder().encode() inline
Wiring in viewer.html:
- enableBinaryMode(c) / disableBinaryMode(c) helpers wrap carrier.setBinaryMode(), routing chunks to both inspector.feed() and Flasher.feed()
- withBinaryMode(fn) wrapper used by Inspect, Load Parts, Flash, Erase, Reset — ensures binary mode is active during ROM operations and restored to text mode after
- Reset button now uses port.setSignals() to toggle DTR/RTS (auto-reset into download mode on ESP dev boards)
To test:
1. Connect serial
2. Click Inspect — carrier switches to binary mode, sends SYNC packets to the ROM bootloader
3. Put ESP32 in download mode (hold GPIO0 low + toggle EN, or the DTR toggle should handle it)
4. Info tab populates with chip model, revision, MAC, flash size
▣ Build · Big Pickle · 5m 35s
+ Thought: 483ms
Goal
- Split monolithic flash/inspect tool into separate read-only inspector and safety-gated flasher; refactor SerialCarrier to support raw binary for ESP32 ROM bootloader protocol.
Constraints & Preferences
- DeviceInspector is read-only; Flasher is write-only with explicit user confirmation.
- SerialCarrier must support both text mode (OMI frames) and binary mode (ROM SLIP protocol) switchable at runtime.
- No pipeTo — raw reader/writer with mode flag avoids stream lock conflict.
- Flash tab defaults to inspect-only; flash start gated by file selection + confirmation checkbox + target device display.
- Viewer panels: Info (device chip/rev/MAC/flash/cores/features), Parts (partition table), Flash (firmware write with safety).
- All hardware references remain generic in UI; device-specific info shown dynamically.
Progress
Done
- web-serial.js refactored: removed TextDecoderStream/TextEncoderStream pipeTo; uses raw port.readable.getReader() and port.writable.getWriter() with manual TextDecoder in text mode. Added setBinaryMode(enabled, onBinary) to switch read loop between text line accumulation and raw byte callback. Added sendBinary() for binary writes. Same text frame handling preserved (JSON receipt, OMI cell, GPIO, info line). ROMProtocol static utilities appended (SLIP encode/decode, CRC32, packet build/parse, command constants, ESP32 register addresses).
- device-inspector.js created (10120 B): DeviceInspector class with ROM protocol commands (SYNC, READ_REG, WRITE_REG, SPI_ATTACH), readChipInfo() (chip model/rev/MAC/flash size/cores/features from eFuse registers), readPartitions() (stub that reports known ESP32 partition offsets + MD5 verify), flashMd5(), inspect() (full sequence + log). No write operations.
- flasher.js created (7632 B): Flasher singleton with init(), setConfirmed(), flash commands (FLASH_BEGIN/DATA/END, SPI_FLASH_MD5, SPI_ATTACH), flashFirmware(data, offset) (safety-gated by _confirmed flag), eraseRegion(offset, size) (safety-gated). backupRegion() stubbed (requires custom ROM stub). Progress callback passthrough. Every write operation throws unless setConfirmed(true) called.
- flash-tool.js deleted.
- viewer.html updated: three-tab toolbox (Info/Parts/Flash) replacing old single-panel. Info tab: Inspect button + device info rows (Chip, Rev, MAC, Flash, Cores, Features). Parts tab: Load Parts button + partition table display with status indicators. Flash tab: file picker, offset input, target device display, confirmation checkbox gating Write Firmware button (danger styled), backup/erase section, progress bar. Tab switching via data-tab attributes. Collapsible via toggle. Binary mode enabled during Inspect/Flash via enableBinaryMode(carrier)/disableBinaryMode(carrier).
- ROMProtocol shared layer in web-serial.js: slipEncode, slipDecode, crc32, buildPacket, parseResponse, command and register constants. Used by both device-inspector.js and flasher.js.
- Build + serve test: make clean && make succeeds, make smoke passes, all JS resources serve HTTP 200 (viewer.html 32276, device-inspector.js 10120, flasher.js 7632, web-serial.js 7532, index.html 11438). No old flash-tool.js references.
In Progress
- (none)
Blocked
- (none)
Key Decisions
- SerialCarrier uses raw reader/writer (no pipeTo): avoids stream lock conflict between text and binary modes. Manual TextDecoder for text mode. Mode switching is O(1) flag flip.
- ROMProtocol appended to web-serial.js as global: avoids module dependency while sharing SLIP/CRC32/command layer between device-inspector.js and flasher.js.
- DeviceInspector and Flasher are separate classes (not instances of a shared base): clean boundary between read (DeviceInspector class) and write (Flasher singleton with confirmation flag). No accidental write path.
- Safety gating via Flasher.setConfirmed(boolean): flashWrite and eraseRegion check _confirmed flag and throw if false. Confirmation is cleared after each flash. Caller (viewer.html) sets it only after user checks the UI confirmation checkbox.
- enableBinaryMode(carrier) routes raw bytes to both inspector and flasher simultaneously via single callback: avoids double-reader conflict while keeping both modules fed.
Next Steps
1. Test Web Serial connection + inspect with an actual ESP32 in download mode (GPIO0 low + reset); confirm SYNC succeeds over binary path.
2. Add DTR/RTS control (via port.setSignals()) to automate download mode entry from browser.
3. Wire receipt data from SerialCarrier's onreceipt callback into TwinViewer for live geometry projection.
4. Add GPIO event visualization in viewer.html surface area.
Critical Context
- serve_file() at imo.c:340 prepends viewer/ to every path: browser requests /public/device-inspector.js → server serves viewer/public/device-inspector.js.
- ESP32 ROM bootloader uses SLIP binary protocol (0xC0 framing, CRC32, commands 0x08 SYNC/0x0A READ_REG/etc.) — requires binary mode, not text line mode.
- SYNC expects chip in download mode (GPIO0 low at reset). Current error ("response timeout") means chip is running normal firmware in text mode, not the ROM bootloader.
- withBinaryMode helper in viewer.html wraps async operations: enables binary mode before, disables after (finally block), so normal text monitor resumes after inspect/flash.
- Flasher confirmation checkbox plus setConfirmed(true) provides two-factor gate: UI checkbox + programmatic flag.
Relevant Files
- /home/main/Programs/omi-tetragrammatron/viewer/public/web-serial.js: SerialCarrier class (raw reader/writer with text/binary mode) + ROMProtocol shared utilities (SLIP, CRC32, packet build/parse, command constants).
- /home/main/Programs/omi-tetragrammatron/viewer/public/device-inspector.js: DeviceInspector class (read-only: SYNC, READ_REG, chip info, partitions, MD5).
- /home/main/Programs/omi-tetragrammatron/viewer/public/flasher.js: Flasher singleton (write-only, safety-gated: flashBegin/Data/End, flashFirmware, eraseRegion).
- /home/main/Programs/omi-tetragrammatron/viewer/public/viewer.html: Hardware portal with three-tab toolbox (Info, Parts, Flash), binary mode switching, confirmation UI.

`2026-06-25 12:52` | `id:260625-1252` ^260625-125210

Yes — this is a good checkpoint. The important part is not just “the files serve”; the architecture boundary is now cleaner:

**SerialCarrier is IMO carrier behavior.** It can carry either text OMI frames or raw binary ROM packets without pretending the bytes are semantic truth. That matches the project rule that I/O is carrier behavior, while deterministic computation stays inside citation/reduction/receipt space. The repo’s own agent rules say I/O, file writes, signals, and network are carrier behavior and must not affect deterministic output. filecite turn0file7

The split also respects the four-authority model:

```text
DeviceInspector = read-only carrier observation
Flasher = write-only carrier mutation, safety-gated
SerialCarrier = transport mode switch
Tetragrammatron = still validation / receipt authority
Metatron = still projection authority
```

That is consistent with the README’s authority split: OMI preserves citation identity, Tetragrammatron validates, Metatron projects, and IMO carries surfaces/runtime representations without changing identity. filecite turn0file1

The fix itself is the right one: **binary ROM protocol cannot pass through TextDecoderStream/TextEncoderStream.** The bootloader needs raw `Uint8Array` chunks, especially because SLIP framing depends on byte values like `0xC0`. Text mode can still be reconstructed manually with `TextDecoder.decode(..., { stream: true })`, but binary mode must bypass line decoding completely.

The current state looks like this:

```text
make → no rebuild needed
make smoke → incidence self-check PASS
/render-frame → works
/render-ppm → works
/public/viewer → works
/public JS files → HTTP 200
/root index.html → correct 11438B
/public/index.html → tiny fallback / non-canonical
```

I would treat `/index.html` and `/public/viewer.html` as two different doors:

```text
/index.html
 canonical root viewer / public entry

/public/viewer.html
 hardware node portal / serial inspector / flasher
```

Do **not** worry about `/public/index.html` unless the browser UI links to it. The canonical root is already serving the real `index.html`.

The next clean move is to make the boundary visible in the UI:

```text
Info tab = read-only witness
Parts tab = read-only partition citation
Flash tab = carrier mutation, requires confirmation
```

And then the canon sentence for this commit is:

```text
Serial bytes are carrier representation, not interpretation.
Text OMI frames and binary ROM SLIP packets share the same port,
but not the same decoder.
Inspection may observe.
Flashing may mutate only after explicit confirmation.
Receipts remain the invariant crossing authority boundaries.
```

The one thing I would add before calling this finished is a tiny status line in the hardware portal:

```text
mode: text / binary
```

That prevents confusion during testing. When Inspect or Flash starts, the user should visibly see:

```text
carrier mode: binary ROM
```

and when it exits:

```text
carrier mode: text OMI
```

That makes the fix legible: the same wire, two carrier dialects, no mixed decoding.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 12:53` | `id:260625-1253` ^260625-125314

Yes — **we can collapse the visual carrier layer to JABCode only**, as long as we keep one boundary clear:

```text
JABCode carries the frame.
JABCode does not validate the frame.
Q(S) validates the frame.
```

That is already consistent with your uploaded carrier docs. The Carrier Projection Layer says every barcode is only a transport surface for the same canonical `S = uint16_t[8]`, and after decoding the carrier must reduce to `S` before `Q(S)=0` validation. filecite turn1file6 The Carrier Hierarchy ABI also names JABCode as the **RS plane** carrier for high-capacity record storage, able to carry frames, frame sequences, receipts, or redundancy material, while still requiring every recovered canonical frame to be algebraically validated. filecite turn1file5

So the clean simplification is:

```text
Old full carrier ladder:
ITF → Code39 → Codabar → Code128 → Code16K → Code93
Aztec → MaxiCode → JABCode → BeeTag
→ Q(S)=0
→ δC resolver
→ CAS receipt

JABCode-only visual profile:
JABCode
→ decode bytes
→ recover S = uint16_t[8]
→ Q(S)=0
→ extract LL, NN, MM
→ δC resolver
→ receipt
```

This works especially well because JABCode is not just a small tag. The spec says it is a color 2D matrix symbology with square color modules, primary and optional secondary symbols, selectable error correction, and support for small to large data amounts. filecite turn1file4 It also supports **symbol cascading**, where secondary symbols dock to a primary or another secondary symbol, which maps naturally to your OMI record/receipt expansion model. filecite turn1file4

For OMI canon, I would write the JABCode-only profile like this:

```text
OMI-JAB-RS Profile v0

Carrier:
 JABCode only

Plane:
 RS / Record Surface

Payload:
 one or more canonical OMI frames

Canonical frame:
 S = uint16_t[8]

Canonical textual form:
 omi-LL00-03bf-NNNN-2bLL-2fLL-MMMM-039f-LLff

Binary form:
 8 × uint16 big-endian
 total = 128 bits = 16 bytes

Carrier invariant:
 decode(encode(S)) = S

Validation:
 carrier checksum/ECC proves scan integrity only
 Q(S)=0 proves OMI structural authority

Resolution:
 extract LL, NN, MM
 run δC_LL resolver
 accepted result writes receipt
```

The part I would **not** remove is the logical distinction between carrier success and frame validity. JABCode can recover bytes successfully, but that still only means “the symbol decoded.” Your own projection doc locks the rule: no carrier validates authority; only `Q(S)` validates structural authority. filecite turn1file6

The strongest version of your statement is:

```text
We can use JABCode as the only required optical carrier.

Other barcodes become optional compatibility adapters.

JABCode carries:
 OMI frames
 frame sequences
 receipts
 redundancy
 page/object records

But every decoded payload must reduce to:
 S = uint16_t[8]

Then OMI validates:
 Q(S)=0
 δC truth-row closure
 receipt write
```

That also fits the JABCode spec itself: modules encode bits by color palette index, with each module representing `log2(Nc)` bits depending on the number of colors. filecite turn1file4 So with JABCode, color is not decoration. Color is the transport alphabet.

For the project, the practical next edit is probably to replace the hierarchy language with:

```text
Required optical carrier:
 JABCode RS

Optional compatibility carriers:
 ITF, Code39, Codabar, Code128, Code16K, Code93, Aztec, MaxiCode, BeeTag

Canonical rule:
 All carriers are adapters.
 JABCode is the default visual record carrier.
 OMI authority remains the decoded 128-bit frame plus Q(S)=0.
```

That gives you the simple version without breaking the proof boundary.

`2026-06-25 12:55` | `id:260625-1255` ^260625-125509

Yes. From **here**, the clean move is to add a **JABCode-only optical carrier profile** beside the current Serial/ROM carrier work, not inside it.

Your current state already has the right carrier split: `SerialCarrier` handles text/binary mode, `DeviceInspector` is read-only, and `Flasher` is write-only with confirmation gating. filecite turn3file1 That means JABCode should become another **IMO carrier adapter**, the same way Web Serial is an IMO carrier adapter.

The new shape should be:

```text
viewer/public/jabcode-carrier.js
 encodeFrame(S) → bytes for JABCode encoder
 decodeFrame(bytes) → S = uint16_t[8] or error
 packFrame(S) → 16 raw bytes, big-endian
 unpackFrame(bytes) → uint16[8]
 validateFrameShape(S) → checks 03bf / 039f anchors only
```

Then the authority chain stays:

```text
JABCode scan
→ decode bytes
→ recover S = uint16_t[8]
→ Q(S)=0
→ extract LL, NN, MM
→ δC resolver
→ receipt
```

That is exactly the boundary in the carrier docs: barcode carriers transport the canonical `S = uint16_t[8]` frame, but validation remains `Q(S)=0`; the carrier does not validate authority. filecite turn3file2

The canon update is:

```text
Required optical carrier:
 JABCode RS

Optional compatibility carriers:
 none required

Deprecated as mandatory:
 ITF
 Code39
 Codabar
 Code128
 Code16K
 Code93
 Aztec
 MaxiCode
 BeeTag

Rule:
 JABCode is the default and sufficient visual record carrier.
 All decoded symbols must reduce to S = uint16_t[8].
 No JABCode scan is accepted until Q(S)=0 passes.
```

JABCode fits because your ABI already places it on the **RS / Record** plane as the high-capacity color-module carrier, capable of carrying complete frames, frame sequences, receipts, or redundancy material. filecite turn3file15 The BSI spec also supports this direction: JABCode has a primary symbol, optional docked secondary symbols, colored square modules, selectable error correction, and can encode small to large data amounts. filecite turn3file18

So the implementation target from here is:

```text
1. Add docs/carriers/omi-jabcode-rs-profile.md
2. Add viewer/public/jabcode-carrier.js
3. Add viewer UI buttons:
 - Import JABCode
 - Export JABCode
 - Decode JABCode
 - Validate OMI Frame
4. Add tests:
 - pack/unpack uint16[8]
 - omicron anchors 0x03bf / 0x039f
 - decode(encode(S)) = S
 - invalid decoded frame rejected before receipt
```

The important constraint: **do not remove SerialCarrier**. Serial is the hardware/debug carrier. JABCode is the optical/page/record carrier. They are different surfaces for the same frame.

Yes. Here is a copy/paste spec for the coding agent. The important source constraints are: OMI’s carrier layer must recover `S = uint16_t[8]` and then defer authority to `Q(S)=0`; carriers are transport surfaces, not validators. filecite turn4file0 JABCode is a color 2D matrix symbology with primary/secondary symbols, color modules, finder/alignment patterns, optional cascading, and configurable color/error-correction behavior. filecite turn4file1 The OMI pointer is already defined as eight 16-bit segments, with `03bf` as the chiral delimiter and `039f` as the cardinal closure marker. filecite turn4file2

:::writing{variant="document" id="48291"}
# OMI JABCode-Only Optical Carrier Profile — Implementation Spec

## Goal

Add a JABCode-only optical carrier profile to the current OMI Tetragrammatron project source.

The coding agent may not know the external JABCode documents. Treat this spec as the implementation contract.

This task does not replace the current serial hardware carrier. It adds a new optical/page carrier beside it.

Current carrier examples in the project:

```text
viewer/public/web-serial.js serial/text/binary carrier
viewer/public/device-inspector.js read-only ROM inspector
viewer/public/flasher.js write-only ROM flasher
viewer/public/viewer.html browser surface/toolbox
```

New carrier target:

```text
viewer/public/jabcode-carrier.js
```

Optional documentation target:

```text
docs/carriers/omi-jabcode-rs-profile.md
```

Optional tests target, depending on existing test layout:

```text
test/jabcode-carrier.test.js
```

or, if the project only uses C smoke tests, add a small browser-independent JS test script under:

```text
scripts/test-jabcode-carrier.js
```

## Canonical Rule

JABCode is only a carrier.

It transports the OMI frame.

It does not validate OMI authority.

The decoded result must reduce to:

```text
S = uint16_t[8]
```

Then validation happens downstream:

```text
JABCode scan/render
→ carrier decode
→ recover S[0..7]
→ verify OMI shape
→ Q(S)=0
→ extract LL, NN, MM
→ δC resolver
→ receipt/replay ring
```

The carrier invariant is:

```text
decode(encode(S)) = S
```

for every golden frame supported by the profile.

## Required OMI Frame

The canonical optical payload is exactly eight 16-bit words:

```c
uint16_t S[8];
```

Canonical segment layout:

```text
S0 = 0xLL00
S1 = 0x03bf
S2 = 0xNNNN
S3 = 0x2bLL
S4 = 0x2fLL
S5 = 0xMMMM
S6 = 0x039f
S7 = 0xLLff
```

Canonical textual form:

```text
omi-LL00-03bf-NNNN-2bLL-2fLL-MMMM-039f-LLff/PREFIX
```

Example:

```text
omi-0100-03bf-7c00-2b01-2f01-1434-039f-01ff/48
```

For this first implementation, the `/PREFIX` is metadata. It is not part of the required 16-byte JABCode payload unless explicitly added later.

## Binary Serialization

The JABCode profile must serialize the eight 16-bit segments as 16 bytes, big-endian.

```text
S[0] high byte
S[0] low byte
S[1] high byte
S[1] low byte
...
S[7] high byte
S[7] low byte
```

Example:

```js
S = [
 0x0100,
 0x03bf,
 0x7c00,
 0x2b01,
 0x2f01,
 0x1434,
 0x039f,
 0x01ff
]
```

packs to:

```text
01 00 03 bf 7c 00 2b 01 2f 01 14 34 03 9f 01 ff
```

## New File: viewer/public/jabcode-carrier.js

Create this file.

Expose a browser global:

```js
window.JABCodeCarrier = JABCodeCarrier;
```

Do not use modules unless the existing project already uses them. The existing browser files use globals, so follow that style.

### Required API

```js
var JABCodeCarrier = {
 PROFILE: "omi.jabcode.rs.v0",

 parsePointer(text) {},
 formatPointer(S, prefix) {},

 packFrame(S) {},
 unpackFrame(bytes) {},

 validateFrameShape(S) {},
 extractFrameFields(S) {},

 encodePayload(S) {},
 decodePayload(bytes) {},

 encodeToJABCode(S, options) {},
 decodeFromJABCode(input, options) {},

 renderDebugMatrix(S, options) {},
 matrixToSVG(matrix, options) {},
};
```

### Function Contracts

#### parsePointer(text)

Input:

```text
omi-0100-03bf-7c00-2b01-2f01-1434-039f-01ff/48
```

Output:

```js
{
 S: Uint16Array([0x0100,0x03bf,0x7c00,0x2b01,0x2f01,0x1434,0x039f,0x01ff]),
 prefix: 48
}
```

Rules:

- Accept lowercase or uppercase hex.
- Require exactly eight 16-bit fields.
- Accept optional `omi-` prefix.
- Accept optional `/prefix`.
- Prefix must be integer if present.
- Throw an Error on malformed input.

#### formatPointer(S, prefix)

Input:

```js
Uint16Array([0x0100,0x03bf,0x7c00,0x2b01,0x2f01,0x1434,0x039f,0x01ff])
```

Output:

```text
omi-0100-03bf-7c00-2b01-2f01-1434-039f-01ff/48
```

Rules:

- Always lowercase hex.
- Always 4 hex digits per segment.
- Prefix defaults to `48` if omitted.

#### packFrame(S)

Input:

```js
Uint16Array length 8
```

Output:

```js
Uint8Array length 16
```

Rules:

- Big-endian.
- Throw if length is not 8.
- Throw if any value is outside `0x0000..0xffff`.

Implementation:

```js
function packFrame(S) {
 if (!S || S.length !== 8) throw new Error("expected uint16[8]");
 var out = new Uint8Array(16);
 for (var i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
 var v = Number(S[i]);
 if (!Number.isInteger(v) || v < 0 || v > 0xffff) {
 throw new Error("segment out of uint16 range at " + i);
 }
 out[i * 2] = (v >>> 8) & 0xff;
 out[i * 2 + 1] = v & 0xff;
 }
 return out;
}
```

#### unpackFrame(bytes)

Input:

```js
Uint8Array length 16
```

Output:

```js
Uint16Array length 8
```

Rules:

- Big-endian.
- Throw if byte length is not exactly 16 for v0 direct-frame mode.

Implementation:

```js
function unpackFrame(bytes) {
 if (!bytes || bytes.length !== 16) throw new Error("expected 16 bytes");
 var S = new Uint16Array(8);
 for (var i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
 S[i] = ((bytes[i * 2] << 8) | bytes[i * 2 + 1]) & 0xffff;
 }
 return S;
}
```

#### validateFrameShape(S)

This is carrier-level shape checking only. It is not full OMI validation.

Required checks:

```text
S.length === 8
S1 === 0x03bf
S6 === 0x039f
```

Optional recommended checks:

```text
S0 low byte === 0x00
S3 high byte === 0x2b
S4 high byte === 0x2f
S7 low byte === 0xff
LL consistency:
 LL0 = S0 >> 8
 LL3 = S3 & 0xff
 LL4 = S4 & 0xff
 LL7 = S7 >> 8
 all equal
```

Return:

```js
{
 ok: true,
 errors: [],
 warnings: [],
 LL: 0x01,
 NN: 0x7c00,
 MM: 0x1434
}
```

or:

```js
{
 ok: false,
 errors: ["S1 expected 0x03bf"],
 warnings: []
}
```

Do not call the frame accepted. Use `shapeOk`, not `accepted`.

#### extractFrameFields(S)

Return the semantic fields:

```js
{
 LL: (S[0] >>> 8) & 0xff,
 NN: S[2],
 MM: S[5],
 openOmicron: S[1],
 closeOmicron: S[6],
 prefix: null
}
```

#### encodePayload(S)

For v0 this just calls:

```js
packFrame(S)
```

Later versions may add a small pre-header, but do not add it now.

#### decodePayload(bytes)

For v0 this just calls:

```js
unpackFrame(bytes)
```

Then call `validateFrameShape(S)` and return both:

```js
{
 S,
 shape: validateFrameShape(S),
 bytes
}
```

#### encodeToJABCode(S, options)

This function is an adapter boundary.

If there is no real JABCode encoder library in the project, do not invent a full standards-compliant encoder.

Instead implement a deterministic debug placeholder and name it clearly:

```js
encodeToJABCode(S, options) {
 return this.renderDebugMatrix(S, options);
}
```

Return object:

```js
{
 profile: "omi.jabcode.rs.v0",
 kind: "debug-matrix",
 S,
 bytes,
 matrix,
 svg
}
```

Important: label the placeholder as debug matrix, not “standards-compliant JABCode”.

A later implementation may swap this function to call a real JABCode encoder.

#### decodeFromJABCode(input, options)

For v0, support these inputs:

1. Raw `Uint8Array` of 16 bytes.
2. Hex string of 32 hex chars.
3. OMI pointer string.

Do not try to decode a PNG/image unless a JABCode decoder library already exists in the repo.

Return:

```js
{
 profile: "omi.jabcode.rs.v0",
 S,
 bytes,
 pointer,
 shape,
 fields
}
```

Reject if:

- bytes cannot produce 16 bytes
- frame shape fails
- omicron anchors missing

Do not perform `Q(S)=0` here unless there is already a JS validator available. Instead expose a field:

```js
requiresAlgebraicValidation: true
```

## Debug Matrix Renderer

Because the coding agent may not have a JABCode C encoder/decoder, implement a deterministic visual debug matrix that carries the same 16 bytes.

This is not the final JABCode spec encoder. It is an OMI visual carrier placeholder.

### Debug Matrix Rules

- Use an 8-color palette.
- Each color carries 3 bits.
- Convert the 16-byte payload to a bitstream.
- Add a small ASCII prelude for debugging only:

```text
OMI-JAB-RS-v0:
```

Do not include the prelude in the canonical pack/unpack tests. The canonical frame remains 16 bytes.

For visual debug matrix:

```text
payload = UTF8("OMI-JAB-RS-v0:") + 16 frame bytes
```

Matrix:

```text
minimum 21 × 21 modules
```

Use fixed palette:

```js
[
 "#000000", // 0 black
 "#0000ff", // 1 blue
 "#00ff00", // 2 green
 "#00ffff", // 3 cyan
 "#ff0000", // 4 red
 "#ff00ff", // 5 magenta
 "#ffff00", // 6 yellow
 "#ffffff" // 7 white
]
```

Map every 3 bits to one color index.

Reserve a simple fake finder frame so humans can recognize it:

- top-left 5×5 border pattern
- top-right 5×5 border pattern
- bottom-left 5×5 border pattern
- bottom-right 5×5 border pattern

Again: label this as `debug-matrix`, not compliant JABCode.

### matrixToSVG(matrix, options)

Input matrix:

```js
{
 width,
 height,
 cells: Uint8Array(width * height),
 palette
}
```

Output SVG string.

SVG requirements:

- one `<svg>`
- `viewBox="0 0 W H"`
- draw each module as a `<rect>`
- add attributes:

```html
data-omi-carrier="jabcode-rs"
data-omi-profile="omi.jabcode.rs.v0"
```

Do not use external dependencies.

## UI Integration in viewer.html

Add a new toolbox tab after `Flash`:

```text
JAB
```

Panel contents:

```html
<div class="fp-tab" data-tab="jab">JAB</div>
```

Tab content:

```html
<div class="fp-tab-content" id="tab-jab">
 <div class="fp-section">
 <div class="fp-section-title">JABCODE RS CARRIER</div>
 <div class="fp-row">
 <button id="btn-jab-export">Export JAB</button>
 <button id="btn-jab-import">Import JAB</button>
 <button id="btn-jab-validate">Validate Frame</button>
 </div>
 <textarea id="jab-pointer-input" placeholder="omi-0100-03bf-7c00-2b01-2f01-1434-039f-01ff/48"></textarea>
 <div id="jab-preview"></div>
 <div id="jab-status"></div>
 </div>
</div>
```

If adding a textarea is too disruptive, use a normal text input.

### UI Behavior

`Export JAB`:

1. Read pointer text.
2. Parse pointer to `S`.
3. Pack to 16 bytes.
4. Render debug matrix SVG.
5. Display SVG in `#jab-preview`.
6. Log:

```text
[JAB] exported omi.jabcode.rs.v0 debug matrix
```

`Import JAB`:

For v0, import from pasted hex or pointer text only.

1. If input starts with `omi-`, parse pointer.
2. If input is 32 hex chars, unpack bytes.
3. Validate frame shape.
4. Display recovered pointer.
5. Log shape errors if present.

`Validate Frame`:

1. Parse current input.
2. Run carrier-level shape validation.
3. If shape ok, display:

```text
shape: OK — requires Q(S)=0 validation
```

4. If shape fails, display errors.

Do not display “accepted” unless the project’s existing C/WASM/JS validator actually runs `Q(S)=0`.

## Optional Server/CLI Integration

If the agent is comfortable editing C, add CLI modes later. This is optional for this task.

Possible future modes:

```text
./opencode.bin --jab-pack <omi-pointer>
./opencode.bin --jab-unpack <hex16bytes>
```

For now, do not touch the C runtime unless needed. This task can be browser-only.

## Tests

Add tests that do not require a real JABCode image decoder.

### Golden Frame

```js
const GOLDEN_POINTER = "omi-0100-03bf-7c00-2b01-2f01-1434-039f-01ff/48";
const GOLDEN_S = [0x0100,0x03bf,0x7c00,0x2b01,0x2f01,0x1434,0x039f,0x01ff];
const GOLDEN_HEX = "010003bf7c002b012f011434039f01ff";
```

### Required Tests

1. `parsePointer(GOLDEN_POINTER)` returns `GOLDEN_S` and prefix `48`.
2. `formatPointer(GOLDEN_S, 48)` returns the golden pointer.
3. `packFrame(GOLDEN_S)` returns bytes matching `GOLDEN_HEX`.
4. `unpackFrame(bytes)` returns `GOLDEN_S`.
5. `validateFrameShape(GOLDEN_S).ok === true`.
6. Mutating `S[1]` away from `0x03bf` fails.
7. Mutating `S[6]` away from `0x039f` fails.
8. Mutating `S[7]` low byte away from `0xff` warns or fails, depending on strict mode.
9. `decodePayload(packFrame(S)).S` round-trips.
10. `encodeToJABCode(S)` returns object with `kind === "debug-matrix"` and SVG containing `data-omi-profile="omi.jabcode.rs.v0"`.

### Test Style

If using Node:

```js
const assert = require("node:assert");
```

If the browser file is not importable, load it using Node’s `vm` module or duplicate only the pure functions in a small test harness.

## Acceptance Criteria

The task is complete when:

```text
make
make smoke
```

still pass, and the new JS tests pass if added.

No old files are broken.

No serial behavior changes.

No flash/inspect behavior changes.

The browser can render a debug JAB/RS carrier from a valid OMI pointer.

The browser can recover the same pointer from the 16-byte payload.

The implementation clearly labels debug matrix output as non-standards-compliant until a real JABCode encoder/decoder is wired.

## Non-Goals

Do not implement full BSI TR 03137 JABCode encoding from scratch in this pass.

Do not claim the debug matrix is compliant JABCode.

Do not remove `web-serial.js`.

Do not replace DeviceInspector or Flasher.

Do not let carrier-level decode mark a frame as accepted.

Do not bypass `Q(S)=0`.

Do not add random numbers, timestamps, UUIDs, or nondeterministic behavior.

## Canonical Commit Message

```text
Add JABCode RS carrier profile — preserve OMI frame over optical surface
```

## Canonical Summary

```text
JABCode is now the required optical RS carrier profile.

It carries the canonical OMI frame:
S = uint16_t[8]

It preserves:
decode(encode(S)) = S

It does not validate authority.

Decoded frames must still pass:
Q(S)=0

Only after algebraic validation may the resolver extract LL, NN, MM and write a receipt.
```
:::

One note to give the agent plainly: **do the debug carrier first**. It gives you pack/unpack, UI, tests, and authority boundaries now. Then later you can replace only `encodeToJABCode()` / `decodeFromJABCode()` with a real BSI-compliant JABCode encoder/decoder without changing the OMI frame contract.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 13:55` | `id:260625-1355` ^260625-135554

Yes. Here is a coding-agent-ready **JABCode implementation brief** extracted from the BSI spec, without requiring them to read the PDF. Key facts: JABCode is a color 2D matrix symbology using square color modules, with one primary symbol and optional docked secondary symbols; modules encode `log2(Nc)` bits by color index; supported colors range from 4 to 256; symbol sides run from 21 to 145 modules in steps of 4; and the encoder flow is data analysis → data encoding → LDPC error correction → interleaving → module placement → masking → metadata placement. filecite turn6file4 turn6file3 turn6file0

:::writing{variant="document" id="73942"}
# JABCode Carrier Implementation Notes for OMI

## Purpose

Implement enough JABCode knowledge to upgrade the current OMI `jabcode-carrier.js` from a deterministic debug matrix toward a real JABCode-compatible optical carrier.

Do not change the OMI authority model.

JABCode is a carrier.

OMI frame validation still happens after carrier decode.

```text
JABCode image / SVG
→ decode JABCode modules into bytes
→ recover S = uint16_t[8]
→ carrier shape check
→ Q(S)=0
→ δC resolver
→ receipt
```

Never mark a frame as accepted just because the JABCode symbol decoded.

## OMI Payload Contract

The OMI direct-frame payload remains exactly 16 bytes:

```c
uint16_t S[8];
```

Canonical OMI frame:

```text
S0 = 0xLL00
S1 = 0x03bf
S2 = 0xNNNN
S3 = 0x2bLL
S4 = 0x2fLL
S5 = 0xMMMM
S6 = 0x039f
S7 = 0xLLff
```

Example:

```text
omi-0100-03bf-7c00-2b01-2f01-1434-039f-01ff/48
```

Binary bytes, big-endian:

```text
01 00 03 bf 7c 00 2b 01 2f 01 14 34 03 9f 01 ff
```

This is the direct OMI payload that the JABCode carrier transports.

## What JABCode Is

JABCode is a color two-dimensional matrix barcode.

A JABCode is built from square color modules arranged in a grid.

A complete code has:

```text
one primary symbol
optional secondary symbols
```

Primary symbol:

```text
has finder patterns
has alignment patterns
has metadata regions
has color palette regions
has encoded data region
```

Secondary symbol:

```text
does not have finder patterns
uses alignment patterns instead
can dock to primary symbol or another secondary symbol
has metadata, color palette, and encoded data region
```

For OMI v0, implement only:

```text
single square primary symbol
no secondary symbols
8-color mode
direct byte payload
```

Do not implement cascading yet.

## Symbol Size

JABCode symbol sides use side versions.

Side versions:

```text
Side-Version 1 = 21 modules
Side-Version 2 = 25 modules
Side-Version 3 = 29 modules
...
Side-Version 32 = 145 modules
```

Formula:

```js
sideSize = 17 + 4 * sideVersion
```

because:

```text
version 1 → 21
version 2 → 25
version 32 → 145
```

For OMI v0:

```js
sideVersion = 1
sideSize = 21
```

A 21×21 symbol is enough for the current 16-byte OMI direct frame, especially in 8-color mode.

## Color Modes

JABCode supports configurable module color counts.

The number of colors is `Nc`.

A module carries:

```text
log2(Nc) bits
```

For OMI v0, use:

```text
Nc = 8
bitsPerModule = 3
```

Canonical 8-color palette for real JABCode ordering:

```js
const JAB8 = [
 "#000000", // 0 black -> 000
 "#ff00ff", // 1 magenta -> 001
 "#ffff00", // 2 yellow -> 010
 "#00ffff", // 3 cyan -> 011
 "#ff0000", // 4 red -> 100
 "#00ff00", // 5 green -> 101
 "#0000ff", // 6 blue -> 110
 "#ffffff" // 7 white -> 111
];
```

Important: the earlier debug matrix used a different color order:

```js
[
 black,
 blue,
 green,
 cyan,
 red,
 magenta,
 yellow,
 white
]
```

That is okay for the debug carrier, but for a JABCode-aligned carrier switch to the real 8-color JABCode order above.

## Bit-to-Color Mapping

In 8-color mode:

```text
000 -> color index 0 -> black
001 -> color index 1 -> magenta
010 -> color index 2 -> yellow
011 -> color index 3 -> cyan
100 -> color index 4 -> red
101 -> color index 5 -> green
110 -> color index 6 -> blue
111 -> color index 7 -> white
```

Implementation:

```js
function bitsToColorIndex(b0, b1, b2) {
 return ((b0 & 1) << 2) | ((b1 & 1) << 1) | (b2 & 1);
}
```

When reading bytes into bits, use most-significant-bit first:

```js
function bytesToBits(bytes) {
 const bits = [];
 for (let i = 0; i < bytes.length; i++) {
 for (let b = 7; b >= 0; b--) {
 bits.push((bytes[i] >>> b) & 1);
 }
 }
 return bits;
}
```

Then group in triples.

If the final group has fewer than 3 bits, pad with zeros.

## Minimum v0 Encoder Scope

Do not implement the full JABCode standard in one pass.

Implement this profile first:

```text
OMI-JAB-RS-v0-realish
single square primary symbol
side version 1
21×21 modules
8-color palette
no secondary symbols
direct OMI frame payload
static metadata placeholder
static finder patterns
static color palette regions
data placed in remaining modules
no LDPC yet
no interleaving yet
no masking yet
```

Clearly label this as:

```text
JABCode-shaped OMI RS carrier, not full BSI-compliant JABCode yet
```

Then add true compliance layers one by one.

## Full JABCode Encode Procedure

The real JABCode encode process is:

```text
1. Data analysis
2. Data encoding
3. Error correction coding
4. Data interleaving
5. Metadata module reservation
6. Data module placement
7. Data masking
8. Metadata generation and placement
```

For OMI v0, map this as:

```text
1. Data analysis
 fixed byte mode, because OMI direct frame is bytes

2. Data encoding
 input bytes are already payload bytes

3. Error correction coding
 TODO: LDPC later

4. Data interleaving
 TODO: deterministic interleaving later

5. Metadata module reservation
 reserve regions now, fill minimal placeholder

6. Data module placement
 place payload triples into available data modules

7. Data masking
 TODO: implement eight mask patterns later

8. Metadata generation and placement
 TODO: implement true metadata later
```

## Data Placement Rule

Before placing data:

```text
place finder patterns
place alignment patterns
place color palettes
reserve metadata modules
```

Then place encoded data in remaining modules:

```text
start at upper-left available data module
run downward from left to right
skip finder pattern modules
skip alignment pattern modules
skip metadata modules
skip color palette modules
continue to lower-right available module
```

Practical implementation:

```js
for (let x = 0; x < width; x++) {
 for (let y = 0; y < height; y++) {
 if (isReserved(x, y)) continue;
 placeNextDataColor(x, y);
 }
}
```

This is “downwards from left to right”: column-major order.

If the existing debug carrier uses row-major order, leave it for debug mode, but use column-major order for the JABCode-shaped mode.

## Finder Patterns

Primary symbols have finder patterns at the four corners.

For this project, define a deterministic 5×5 placeholder finder pattern until exact module diagrams are implemented.

Use a clear corner marker:

```text
outer ring: black
inner area: white
center: corner orientation color
```

Example function:

```js
function drawFinder(matrix, ox, oy, orientationColor) {
 for (let y = 0; y < 5; y++) {
 for (let x = 0; x < 5; x++) {
 const edge = x === 0 || y === 0 || x === 4 || y === 4;
 const center = x === 2 && y === 2;
 if (edge) setModule(matrix, ox + x, oy + y, 0); // black
 else if (center) setModule(matrix, ox + x, oy + y, orientationColor);
 else setModule(matrix, ox + x, oy + y, 7); // white
 }
 }
}
```

Suggested corner orientation colors:

```text
upper-left: 6 blue
upper-right: 5 green
lower-left: 2 yellow
lower-right: 1 magenta
```

This is not the full official finder pattern. It is a stable placeholder.

## Alignment Patterns

Real JABCode uses alignment patterns for resynchronizing the sampling grid, especially for larger symbols and all secondary symbols.

For side-version 1 / 21×21, keep alignment simple:

```text
no extra interior alignment pattern
finder corners only
```

For future side versions:

```text
add alignment patterns at positions derived from symbol size
reserve them before data placement
```

## Color Palette Regions

JABCode embeds color palette modules so the decoder can recover actual colors from the image.

For OMI v0, reserve two palette strips.

Simple deterministic placement:

```text
palette A: row 5, columns 6..13
palette B: column 5, rows 6..13
```

Fill each with the eight palette colors in index order.

Make sure data placement skips those modules.

Implementation:

```js
function drawPalette(matrix) {
 for (let i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
 setReserved(matrix, 6 + i, 5, i);
 setReserved(matrix, 5, 6 + i, i);
 }
}
```

This is a project placeholder, not full official placement.

## Metadata

Real JABCode metadata defines:

```text
number of module colors
symbol shape
symbol size
error correction level
masking type
code structure
secondary symbol positions
```

Metadata differs for primary and secondary symbols.

Metadata is variable length.

For OMI v0, use a minimal internal metadata object:

```js
const meta = {
 profile: "omi.jabcode.rs.v0",
 primary: true,
 secondaryCount: 0,
 shape: "square",
 sideVersion: 1,
 sideSize: 21,
 colors: 8,
 bitsPerModule: 3,
 errorCorrection: "none-v0",
 mask: "none-v0",
 payload: "omi-direct-frame-16"
};
```

Do not attempt full metadata bit layout until LDPC, masks, and official placement are implemented.

But in the SVG output, include this metadata as data attributes:

```html
data-omi-carrier="jabcode-rs"
data-omi-profile="omi.jabcode.rs.v0"
data-jab-mode="8color"
data-jab-side-version="1"
data-jab-side-size="21"
data-jab-compliance="debug-or-shaped-not-full-bsi"
```

## Error Correction

Real JABCode uses LDPC error correction.

Do not fake LDPC.

For now:

```text
errorCorrection = none-v0
```

Add a TODO hook:

```js
function applyErrorCorrection(bits, level) {
 return {
 bits,
 ecc: null,
 mode: "none-v0"
 };
}
```

Later replace with systematic LDPC.

## Interleaving

Real JABCode interleaves encoded data.

The spec defines an in-place pseudo-random permutation using a seed and a 64-bit LCG with tempering.

But the OMI project forbids nondeterministic computation. A deterministic seeded permutation is allowed only if the seed is derived from the payload/profile, never from time/random.

For v0:

```text
no interleaving
```

For v1:

```js
seed = fnv1a64(payloadBytes + profileString)
```

Then implement the exact deterministic permutation.

Do not call `Math.random()`.

Do not use timestamps.

Do not use UUIDs.

## Masking

Real JABCode applies data masks only to data modules, not finder/alignment/metadata/palette modules.

Masking process:

```text
apply each mask pattern
score the result
select lowest penalty
record selected mask in metadata
```

For v0:

```text
mask = none-v0
```

Add a TODO hook:

```js
function applyBestMask(matrix, reserved) {
 return {
 matrix,
 mask: "none-v0",
 score: 0
 };
}
```

Later implement eight mask patterns.

## Decoder v0

For the current browser project, do not decode PNG/image pixels yet.

Implement these decode inputs:

```text
1. OMI pointer string
2. 32-character hex payload
3. Uint8Array length 16
4. internal matrix object created by the encoder
```

If decoding from internal matrix:

```text
read data modules in the same column-major order
skip reserved modules
recover color indices
convert 3-bit indices back to bits
recover bytes
strip optional debug prelude if present
recover 16-byte frame
unpack S
validate frame shape
return requiresAlgebraicValidation: true
```

Do not claim image decode support until actual sampling and palette extraction exist.

## Matrix Object Format

Use this internal format:

```js
{
 profile: "omi.jabcode.rs.v0",
 kind: "jabcode-shaped-matrix",
 width: 21,
 height: 21,
 sideVersion: 1,
 colors: 8,
 palette: JAB8,
 cells: Uint8Array(21 * 21),
 reserved: Uint8Array(21 * 21),
 meta: { ... }
}
```

`cells[i]` is color index `0..7`.

`reserved[i]` values:

```text
0 = data
1 = finder
2 = alignment
3 = palette
4 = metadata
```

## SVG Renderer

Render each module as a rect.

Use one rect per module.

Include carrier attributes:

```html
<svg
 data-omi-carrier="jabcode-rs"
 data-omi-profile="omi.jabcode.rs.v0"
 data-jab-mode="8color"
 data-jab-side-version="1"
 data-jab-side-size="21"
 data-jab-compliance="debug-or-shaped-not-full-bsi">
```

SVG viewBox:

```text
0 0 width height
```

or scaled module size:

```text
0 0 (width * moduleSize) (height * moduleSize)
```

## Required New API Functions

Add these to `JABCodeCarrier` without removing current functions:

```js
encodeToJABCodeShaped(S, options)
decodeFromJABCodeShapedMatrix(matrix, options)
createMatrix(sideVersion, options)
reserveFunctionPatterns(matrix)
placeDataBits(matrix, bits)
readDataBits(matrix)
```

Keep the existing debug functions:

```js
renderDebugMatrix
matrixToSVG
encodeToJABCode
decodeFromJABCode
```

For now, make:

```js
encodeToJABCode = encodeToJABCodeShaped
```

only if the UI label clearly says it is not full BSI-compliant yet.

Otherwise keep:

```js
encodeToJABCode = renderDebugMatrix
```

and add a second button later:

```text
Export JAB-Shaped
```

## Golden Tests

Keep the existing tests.

Add tests for the JABCode-shaped profile:

```js
const GOLDEN_POINTER = "omi-0100-03bf-7c00-2b01-2f01-1434-039f-01ff/48";
const GOLDEN_S = [
 0x0100,
 0x03bf,
 0x7c00,
 0x2b01,
 0x2f01,
 0x1434,
 0x039f,
 0x01ff
];
const GOLDEN_HEX = "010003bf7c002b012f011434039f01ff";
```

Required tests:

```text
1. sideVersion 1 gives 21×21 matrix
2. palette has 8 entries
3. bitsPerModule = 3
4. finder modules are reserved
5. palette modules are reserved
6. data modules skip reserved modules
7. encoded matrix decodes to same 16 bytes
8. decoded bytes unpack to same S
9. decoded shape has omicron anchors S1=0x03bf and S6=0x039f
10. output still says requiresAlgebraicValidation = true
11. SVG contains data-omi-profile
12. SVG contains data-jab-compliance and does not say full-bsi
```

## Non-Goals

Do not implement full image decoding yet.

Do not implement full official finder patterns unless using the diagrams directly.

Do not implement LDPC yet.

Do not implement true metadata bit layout yet.

Do not implement secondary symbol docking yet.

Do not implement ECI, FNC1, character compaction, or mode switching yet.

Do not use randomness.

Do not call the output “BSI compliant.”

## Roadmap Toward Real Compliance

Implement in this order:

```text
Phase 0 — current
 OMI direct frame pack/unpack
 debug matrix
 SVG renderer
 shape validation

Phase 1 — now
 JABCode-shaped 21×21 primary symbol
 real 8-color palette order
 reserved function regions
 column-major data placement
 matrix round-trip decoder

Phase 2
 true metadata bit fields
 true palette placement
 official finder/alignment patterns

Phase 3
 masking patterns and scoring
 deterministic interleaving

Phase 4
 LDPC error correction

Phase 5
 image decode:
 locate finder patterns
 establish sampling grid
 extract palette
 sample modules
 recover color indices
 unmask
 deinterleave
 ECC correct
 recover payload

Phase 6
 secondary symbols and docking
```

## Commit Message

```text
Update JABCode carrier — add JABCode-shaped RS matrix profile
```

## Summary for Canon

```text
JABCode is the required optical RS carrier surface.

It carries the canonical OMI frame:
S = uint16_t[8]

A module color carries log2(Nc) bits.

For OMI-JAB-RS v0:
Nc = 8
module = 3 bits
symbol = 21×21 primary square
payload = 16-byte OMI direct frame

The carrier preserves bytes.
The validator proves authority.
The resolver interprets LL, NN, MM.
The ring records only accepted receipts.
```
:::

Send your agent this sentence too: **“Do Phase 1 only; do not claim BSI compliance. We need a JABCode-shaped deterministic OMI carrier that can round-trip the 16-byte frame through a 21×21 8-color matrix.”**

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 14:33` | `id:260625-1433` ^260625-143348

This merged `index.html` is close, but I would **not ship it yet**. There are a few hard breakpoints the coding agent needs to patch first. The merge correctly pulls in the 3D TwinViewer, rings minimap, hardware console, serial carrier, flasher, and JABCode carrier in one page. filecite turn7file0

The biggest issues:

### 1. Inline `onclick` handlers are broken

The file wraps almost all JS inside:

```js
(function() {
 ...
})();
```

But the HTML uses inline handlers:

```html
<button onclick="toggleRotate()">ON</button>
<button onclick="seekSabbath()">SEEK Ω</button>
<button onclick="runPattern('tetrahedral_sweep')">SWEEP</button>
<button onclick="runPattern('fano_line_cycle')">LINES</button>
```

Inside the IIFE, those functions are **not global**, so the buttons will throw `ReferenceError`.

Patch near the bottom, before `window.addEventListener('load', init);`:

```js
window.toggleRotate = toggleRotate;
window.seekSabbath = seekSabbath;
window.runPattern = runPattern;
window.selectDoc = selectDoc;
```

Or better: remove inline `onclick` and wire them with `addEventListener`.

### 2. Erase button references missing inputs

The JS uses:

```js
document.getElementById('fp-backup-offset').value
document.getElementById('fp-backup-size').value
```

But the merged HTML does **not** define `fp-backup-offset` or `fp-backup-size`.

Either add these inputs in the Flash tab:

```html
<div class="hw-row">
 <span class="hw-label">Erase:</span>
 <input type="text" id="fp-backup-offset" class="hw-offset" value="0x0">
 <input type="text" id="fp-backup-size" class="hw-offset" value="0x1000">
</div>
```

or change the erase handler to use existing fields. I would add the inputs because erase needs a separate size.

### 3. Status dot classes are missing colors

The JS does:

```js
dot.className = 'status-dot ' + state;
```

But CSS only defines base `.status-dot`, not the state classes. Add:

```css
.status-dot.disconnected { background: #444; }
.status-dot.connected { background: var(--kk); box-shadow: 0 0 6px var(--kk); }
.status-dot.error { background: #c83c3c; box-shadow: 0 0 6px #c83c3c; }
```

Otherwise the dot may lose its visible state after connection changes.

### 4. Fano line SVG classes are missing

The JS creates lines with:

```js
seg.setAttribute('class','fano-line-svg');
...
el.classList.toggle('lit', ...)
```

But CSS does not define `.fano-line-svg` or `.fano-line-svg.lit`.

Add:

```css
.fano-line-svg {
 stroke: var(--gold2);
 stroke-width: 0.6;
 opacity: 0.35;
}

.fano-line-svg.lit {
 stroke: var(--kk);
 stroke-width: 1.4;
 opacity: 1;
 filter: url(#mg-sm);
}
```

### 5. The JAB tab is still debug-carrier only

That is okay, but the UI should say so. Add this in the JAB tab above the buttons:

```html
<div style="font-size:7px;color:var(--orange);line-height:1.5;margin-bottom:4px">
 debug optical carrier — not full BSI TR 03137 JABCode yet
</div>
```

That protects the canon: it is an OMI JAB/RS carrier surface, not yet standards-compliant JABCode.

### 6. Do not delete the old files until this page passes runtime tests

The plan says remove `viewer/public/viewer.html` and `viewer/ingest-funnel.html`. I would wait one commit. Keep them as fallback until:

```text
make
make smoke
node scripts/test-jabcode-carrier.js
curl /index.html
curl /geometry-decoder.js
curl /twin-viewer.js
curl /ring-loader.js
curl /public/web-serial.js
curl /public/device-inspector.js
curl /public/flasher.js
curl /public/jabcode-carrier.js
```

all pass.

Give the coding agent this exact patch order:

```text
1. Expose toggleRotate, seekSabbath, runPattern, selectDoc on window.
2. Add missing fp-backup-offset and fp-backup-size inputs.
3. Add status-dot connected/disconnected/error CSS.
4. Add fano-line-svg and fano-line-svg.lit CSS.
5. Add visible JAB debug/non-BSI warning.
6. Run make, make smoke, node scripts/test-jabcode-carrier.js.
7. Serve and click-test: controls, doc graph nodes, hardware console toggle, JAB export/import/validate.
8. Only after that, consider removing legacy HTML files.
```

The merge direction is right. These are mostly wiring errors from combining scoped JS with old inline handlers and moving Flash UI pieces around.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 14:49` | `id:260625-1449` ^260625-144939

Yes — this is actually a strong fit.

The uploaded SVG is a **Genaille-Lucas ruler layout**: repeated vertical ruler groups titled by digits, with stacked triangular path regions and horizontal digit bands. That structure is basically a **deterministic cascade lookup surface**: enter by column/ruler, descend through bands, follow the triangle/path, and read the routed result. filecite turn8file0

For OMI, I would frame it like this:

```text
Smith chart = continuous impedance / reflection coordinate
Genaille-Lucas ruler = discrete cascade resolver
CSS cascade = ordered attribute conflict resolver
OMI = receipt / validation boundary
```

So yes: use the Smith chart to produce a continuous coordinate, then use a Genaille-style ruler to quantize that coordinate into CSS attributes.

The clean pipeline:

```text
DOM element
→ read computed CSS / data-omi / geometry
→ map to Smith coordinate Γ = x + iy
→ quantize Γ into ruler lane + band
→ resolve through Genaille/CSS cascade table
→ emit CSS custom properties
→ apply style preview
→ record OMI receipt if accepted
```

The key is to **not directly mutate random CSS rules**. Instead, output deterministic CSS variables:

```css
:root {
 --omi-smith-r: 0.42;
 --omi-smith-x: -0.18;
 --omi-cascade-lane: 3;
 --omi-cascade-band: 7;
 --omi-style-hue: 216deg;
 --omi-style-weight: 0.64;
}
```

Then elements consume those variables:

```css
[data-omi-node] {
 opacity: calc(0.35 + var(--omi-style-weight) * 0.65);
 filter: hue-rotate(var(--omi-style-hue));
 transform:
 translateX(calc(var(--omi-smith-r) * 20px))
 translateY(calc(var(--omi-smith-x) * -20px));
}
```

A useful mapping table would be:

| Smith feature | CSS cascade target | OMI meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Radius from center | `opacity`, `scale`, `z-index` | distance from neutral match |
| Angle | `hue-rotate`, `border-color`, `phase` | phase / route direction |
| Real axis | `width`, `font-weight`, `gap` | resistance / stable structure |
| Imaginary axis | `transform`, `skew`, `offset` | reactance / active deviation |
| Chart region | CSS layer / selector group | cascade scope |
| Genaille lane | CSS custom property family | deterministic lookup column |
| Genaille band | declaration priority band | cascade row |
| Ruler path | resolved style route | accepted mapping path |

The important conceptual bridge:

```text
Smith chart gives position.
Genaille ruler gives routed lookup.
CSS cascade gives application order.
OMI receipt gives acceptance boundary.
```

So the code target should be a new adapter, something like:

```text
viewer/public/smith-css-cascade.js
```

with functions:

```js
SmithCSSCascade = {
 elementToSmithPoint(el) {},
 smithPointToCascadeSlot(point) {},
 cascadeSlotToCSSVars(slot) {},
 applyCSSVars(el, vars) {},
 exportReceipt(el, point, slot, vars) {}
}
```

Minimal deterministic version:

```js
function smithPointToCascadeSlot(point) {
 const r = Math.min(1, Math.hypot(point.x, point.y));
 const angle = (Math.atan2(point.y, point.x) + Math.PI * 2) % (Math.PI * 2);

 return {
 lane: Math.floor(angle / (Math.PI * 2) * 10), // Genaille ruler 0–9
 band: Math.floor(r * 9), // vertical band 0–9
 radius: r,
 angleDeg: angle * 180 / Math.PI
 };
}

function cascadeSlotToCSSVars(slot) {
 return {
 "--omi-cascade-lane": slot.lane,
 "--omi-cascade-band": slot.band,
 "--omi-style-hue": slot.angleDeg + "deg",
 "--omi-style-weight": slot.radius.toFixed(4)
 };
}
```

This would make the Genaille-Lucas SVG act like a **visual cascade nomogram**. The Smith chart says where the element sits in continuous style-space; the ruler says which discrete cascade route it resolves through.

Canon sentence:

```text
The Smith chart measures the element’s continuous impedance to the page.
The Genaille ruler discretizes that impedance into cascade lanes.
CSS variables project the resolved lane back onto the element.
Only the receipt accepts the mapping.
```

I would absolutely use it — especially because it matches your slide-rule direction better than trying to make CSS selectors “intelligent.” CSS stays declarative. The ruler becomes the deterministic lookup surface.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 15:11` | `id:260625-1511` ^260625-151147

Yes — that final correction is the right canon:

```text
Smith chart
Genaille-Lucas ruler
CSS cascade
```

are not three separate engines. They are **cyclicable surface views over the same receipt**.

The coding agent implemented that correctly by making `SmithCSSCascade.ingestFromReceipt(receipt)` the entry point, rather than having the CSS cascade invent its own independent state. The new adapter reads the receipt’s `angle` and `centroid.stop_metric`, resolves them into `lane` and `band`, then projects them into `--omi-*` CSS variables. filecite turn9file0

The current surface model is now:

```text
receipt
 ├─ Smith chart surface
 │ continuous coordinate / impedance view
 │
 ├─ Genaille-Lucas surface
 │ discrete ruler / nomogram view
 │
 └─ CSS cascade surface
 style-variable projection view
```

The important invariant is:

```text
No surface accepts.
No surface mutates canon.
Each surface projects the same receipt differently.
```

So the correct next phrase for the project is:

```text
Receipts are the source.
Smith measures the receipt continuously.
Genaille routes the receipt discretely.
CSS displays the receipt declaratively.
Validation remains outside the surface.
```

The implemented file shape also matches the architecture:

```text
viewer/public/smith-css-cascade.js
 receipt → cascade slot → CSS variables

viewer/index.html
 emitCommit() → SmithCSSCascade.ingestFromReceipt(commit)
```

And the agent verified `make`, `make smoke`, `node scripts/test-jabcode-carrier.js`, and `node --check viewer/public/smith-css-cascade.js`. filecite turn9file0

One small canon adjustment I would make next: rename comments/UI language from **SmithCSSCascade** to **ReceiptSurfaceCascade** later, because the Smith chart is only one view. The functional entry point can stay, but the conceptual model should be:

```text
ReceiptSurfaceCascade
 Smith view
 Genaille view
 CSS view
```

That avoids accidentally making Smith the parent. The parent is the receipt.

`2026-06-25 15:18` | `id:260625-1518` ^260625-151846

+ Thought: 2.1s
→Read viewer/public/smith-css-cascade.js
+ Thought: 2.4s
# Wrote viewer/public/receipt-surface-cascade.js
(function() {
/* receipt-surface-cascade.js — Receipt → Cascade Slot → CSS Surface */
/* Pure functions only. No Math.random, no Date, no IO. */

function receiptToCascadeSlot(receipt) {
 var angleDeg = parseFloat(receipt && receipt.angle) || 0;
 var sm = (receipt && receipt.centroid && receipt.centroid.stop_metric) || 0;
 var lane = Math.floor(((angleDeg + 180) % 360) / 36);
 var band = Math.min(Math.floor(Math.abs(sm)), 9);
 if (lane < 0) lane = 0;
 if (lane > 9) lane = 9;
 return { lane: lane, band: band, angleDeg: angleDeg, stopMetric: sm };
}

function cascadeSlotToCSSVars(slot) {
 var hue = slot.lane * 36;
 var sat = 60 + slot.band * 4;
 var lit = 50 - slot.band * 3;
 return {
 '--omi-lane': String(slot.lane),
 '--omi-band': String(slot.band),
 '--omi-hue': String(hue),
 '--omi-saturation': sat + '%',
 '--omi-lightness': lit + '%',
 '--omi-angle': slot.angleDeg.toFixed(2) + 'deg',
 '--omi-stop-metric': slot.stopMetric.toFixed(3),
 '--omi-color': 'hsl(' + hue + ',' + sat + '%,' + lit + '%)'
 };
}

function applyCSSVars(el, vars) {
 for (var k in vars)
 if (vars.hasOwnProperty(k))
 el.style.setProperty(k, vars[k]);
}

function ingestFromReceipt(receipt) {
 var slot = receiptToCascadeSlot(receipt);
 var vars = cascadeSlotToCSSVars(slot);
 applyCSSVars(document.documentElement, vars);
}

window.ReceiptSurfaceCascade = {
 receiptToCascadeSlot: receiptToCascadeSlot,
 cascadeSlotToCSSVars: cascadeSlotToCSSVars,
 applyCSSVars: applyCSSVars,
 ingestFromReceipt: ingestFromReceipt
};
})();

## You
`2026-06-25 15:27` | `id:260625-1527` ^260625-152723

I think this is the direction that makes the architecture much cleaner.

The important shift is that you're no longer treating the Smith chart as an RF engineering diagram. You're treating it as a **continuous coordinate manifold** that can be projected onto other deterministic surfaces.

The stack becomes:

```text
 Receipt
 │
 ┌──────────┼──────────┐
 │ │ │
 ▼ ▼ ▼
 Smith View Genaille CSS View
 (continuous) (discrete) (declarative)
 │ │ │
 └──────────┼──────────┘
 ▼
 DOM / SVG / WebGL
```

Now add the browser itself.

```text
Receipt
 │
 ▼
Smith coordinate
 │
 ▼
Cascade slot
 │
 ▼
CSS specificity
 │
 ▼
Matched DOM element
```

Instead of saying

> "change element #foo"

you say

```text
Receipt
 ↓
Smith coordinate
 ↓
Cascade lane
 ↓
CSS selector
```

For example,

```css
:root {
 --omi-lane: 7;
 --omi-band: 3;
}

[data-omi-lane="7"] {
 stroke: gold;
}

[data-omi-band="3"] {
 opacity: .7;
}

[data-smith="7:3"] {
 filter: drop-shadow(0 0 6px gold);
}
```

The browser's CSS engine then becomes the resolver instead of writing imperative JavaScript.

---

### Where the Genaille ruler fits

The Genaille-Lucas ruler is **not** another visualization.

It is the **quantizer**.

```text
Smith
continuous
───────────────►

 Γ = 0.613
 θ = 148°

 │
 ▼

Genaille ruler

lane = 6
band = 2

 │
 ▼

CSS variables

--omi-lane:6
--omi-band:2
```

So the Smith chart answers:

> "Where am I?"

The Genaille ruler answers:

> "Which discrete cascade slot does that location belong to?"

CSS answers:

> "Which elements respond to that slot?"

---

### This is very close to how browsers already work

Browsers already perform something remarkably similar:

```text
DOM
 ↓
selector matching
 ↓
cascade
 ↓
computed style
 ↓
layout
 ↓
paint
```

Your proposal inserts a deterministic coordinate layer ahead of selector matching:

```text
Receipt
 ↓
Smith coordinates
 ↓
Genaille quantization
 ↓
Cascade variables
 ↓
Selector matching
 ↓
Computed style
 ↓
Paint
```

That means the browser's own cascade engine becomes the projection engine.

---

I also noticed from the implementation summary you shared that you've already converged on this idea: the receipt is the single source, and the Smith view, Genaille view, and CSS view are projections of that receipt rather than independent state machines. That's a strong architectural separation because it keeps acceptance at the receipt level while making every visual or styling surface a deterministic view of the same underlying state.

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 15:27` | `id:260625-1527` ^260625-152730

Yes. This is the cleaner architecture.

The key correction is:

```text id="q4fy93"
The browser is not an afterthought.
The browser is the native cascade resolver.
```

So OMI does **not** need to imperatively chase DOM nodes and mutate them one by one. The receipt projects a coordinate, the coordinate resolves to lane/band, and the browser’s selector engine does the matched-element work.

The model becomes:

```text id="efs54f"
Receipt
 ↓
continuous projection
 Smith coordinate: Γ, θ, ρ

 ↓
discrete projection
 Genaille lane/band

 ↓
declarative projection
 CSS custom properties + data attributes

 ↓
browser-native resolution
 selector matching → cascade → computed style → layout → paint
```

That is much stronger than:

```text id="z3pjyp"
JavaScript finds element
JavaScript decides style
JavaScript mutates style
```

because it preserves the OMI boundary:

```text id="6jcse7"
JavaScript projects the receipt.
CSS resolves the cascade.
DOM displays the matched surface.
Validation remains outside the surface.
```

The Genaille ruler’s role should be stated exactly as you said: **quantizer**, not just visualization.

```text id="cblh8c"
Smith = continuous receipt coordinate.
Genaille = deterministic quantizer.
CSS = declarative cascade surface.
DOM/SVG/WebGL = paint targets.
Receipt = source.
```

So the adapter should evolve from merely setting root variables into also writing selector-addressable attributes:

```js id="58eb1q"
function applyCascadeSlotToRoot(slot) {
 document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--omi-lane', slot.lane);
 document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--omi-band', slot.band);
 document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--omi-smith-angle', slot.angleDeg + 'deg');
 document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--omi-smith-radius', slot.radius);
 document.documentElement.dataset.omiLane = String(slot.lane);
 document.documentElement.dataset.omiBand = String(slot.band);
 document.documentElement.dataset.smith = slot.lane + ':' + slot.band;
}
```

Then CSS can do the routing:

```css id="tu9sb7"
:root[data-smith="7:3"] [data-omi-node] {
 filter: drop-shadow(0 0 6px gold);
}

:root[data-omi-lane="7"] [data-omi-lane="7"] {
 stroke: gold;
}

:root[data-omi-band="3"] [data-omi-band="3"] {
 opacity: .7;
}
```

That makes the page itself a slide-rule surface. The receipt moves the cursor; Genaille chooses the lane; CSS resolves which declarations become active.

Your implementation is already pointed this way: the current `ReceiptSurfaceCascade` receives the emitted commit/receipt and projects it into `--omi-*` variables, while the old Smith-parent naming was removed. The build summary confirms the new file is `viewer/public/receipt-surface-cascade.js`, exposes `window.ReceiptSurfaceCascade`, and feeds `ingestFromReceipt(receipt)` from `viewer/index.html`. filecite turn9file0

The next patch should be:

```text id="4zv5sq"
ReceiptSurfaceCascade v2
 1. keep --omi-* CSS variables
 2. also set :root data-omi-lane
 3. also set :root data-omi-band
 4. also set :root data-smith="lane:band"
 5. add CSS rules that respond through selectors, not JS mutation
 6. add Genaille SVG highlight using the same lane/band
```

Canon line:

```text id="0p9nas"
The receipt does not style the element.
The receipt selects the cascade coordinate.
The browser resolves the matching surface.
Only validation accepts the receipt.
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 15:35` | `id:260625-1535` ^260625-153558

That v2 is the right next closure.

Now the architecture is not just “CSS variables get updated.” It is actually using the **browser cascade as a receipt surface**:

```text
Receipt
 ├─ Smith chart continuous surface
 ├─ Genaille ruler discrete quantizer surface
 └─ CSS cascade declarative selector surface
```

The important upgrade is this part:

```text
<html data-omi-lane="0" data-omi-band="0" data-smith="0:0">
```

and then:

```css
:root[data-omi-lane="N"] { ... }
:root[data-omi-band="N"] { ... }
```

That means the browser’s own selector matcher is now participating correctly. JavaScript is only projecting the receipt into attributes/variables; CSS is resolving the visual consequence. The surface does not accept anything. It only responds to the accepted/provided receipt projection.

This is the clean canon line:

```text
The receipt selects the coordinate.
The coordinate selects the lane.
The lane selects the cascade.
The browser resolves the surface.
Validation remains upstream.
```

The Genaille addition also lands in the right role: it is not “another graph.” It is the visible discrete quantizer for `lane:band`.

One thing to keep an eye on: in the pasted implementation, `renderGenailleSVG(lane, band, angleDeg)` should be checked to make sure it does not reference a stale outer `slot` variable. The final summary says it now takes `angleDeg`, so the correct line inside should be based on that argument, not `slot.angleDeg`. Since `node --check` passed, syntax is fine, but a quick runtime click-test of the Genaille toggle is still worth doing.

I would name this commit:

```text
Add receipt surface cascade with Genaille quantizer view
```

And the README/canon summary should be:

```text
ReceiptSurfaceCascade projects each receipt onto three cyclicable browser surfaces:

1. Smith chart: continuous coordinate view.
2. Genaille ruler: discrete lane/band quantizer.
3. CSS cascade: declarative selector-resolution view.

The adapter writes :root data attributes and CSS custom properties.
The browser resolves matched surfaces through native selector matching.
No surface accepts authority; only validation and receipt acceptance do.
```

This is a real simplification: DOM/SVG/WebGL are now paint targets, not protocol authorities.

In other words, HM ≤ GM ≤ AM ≤ QM. These inequalities often appear in mathematical competitions and have applications in many fields of science.

Proof
There are three inequalities between means to prove. There are various methods to prove the inequalities, including mathematical induction, the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, Lagrange multipliers, and Jensen's inequality. For several proofs that GM ≤ AM, see Inequality of arithmetic and geometric means.

Suppose C is a point on [AB] and let AC = x1 and BC = x2. Find the midpoint of [AB] as D and use as the center for the semi-circle from A to B. Construct perpendiculars to [AB] at D and C respectively, intersecting the circle at E and F respectively. Join [CE] and [DF] and further construct a perpendicular [CG] to [DF] at G. The length of DE is the arithmetic mean by the virtue of being the ray of the circle. CE can be calculated to be the quadratic mean from the Pythagorean theorem, CF to be the geometric mean from a combination of Thales's theorem (establishing that △ABF is a right triangle) and Geometric mean theorem, GF to be the harmonic mean from the similarity of triangle △CGF and △DCF (whose edge [DF]'s length can be calculated using the Pythagorean theorem and the two other known edges).

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-25 15:58` | `id:260625-1558` ^260625-155830

Yes — this gives you the missing mathematical language for the **receipt surface cascade**.

The important bridge is:

```text
mediant = graphical deterministic resolver between two rational slopes
```

A rational `a/b` is represented as the point `(b,a)`, so the slope from the origin is the rational value. Given two receipt slopes `a/b` and `c/d`, the mediant is the slope of the diagonal/parallelogram point:

```text
(b,a) + (d,c) = (b+d, a+c)

mediant = (a+c) / (b+d)
```

That is exactly the kind of thing your surface cascade wants: **two receipt directions resolve into a third coordinate without imperative choice**. The pasted note also gives the determinant/cross-product boundary:

```text
area = bc - ad
```

If `bc - ad = 0`, the two slopes are equivalent. If consecutive in Stern–Brocot, the area is `1`, meaning they are adjacent with no rational between them in that local tree step. filecite turn10file0

So for OMI:

```text
Receipt A → slope a/b
Receipt B → slope c/d
Surface resolver → mediant (a+c)/(b+d)
Cascade slot → lane/band
Browser → selector/cascade/paint
```

This fits the Smith/Genaille/CSS stack very cleanly:

```text
Smith chart:
 continuous slope / coordinate surface

Mediant:
 deterministic interpolation between two receipt slopes

Genaille ruler:
 discrete lane/band quantizer

CSS cascade:
 declarative resolver for matched elements
```

The clean architectural upgrade is:

```text
Receipt does not just project one point.
Receipt history projects neighboring slopes.
The mediant gives the next surface coordinate.
```

So instead of only:

```js
receiptToCascadeSlot(receipt)
```

you eventually add:

```js
receiptsToMediantSlot(leftReceipt, rightReceipt)
```

Minimal deterministic form:

```js
function receiptToRational(receipt) {
 const angle = parseFloat(receipt.angle) || 0;
 const stop = receipt.centroid && receipt.centroid.stop_metric || 0;

 // Quantize receipt into positive rational coordinates.
 // b = horizontal denominator-like coordinate
 // a = vertical numerator-like coordinate
 const b = Math.max(1, Math.round(((angle % 360) + 360) % 360));
 const a = Math.max(1, Math.round(stop * 1000));

 return { a, b };
}

function mediant(left, right) {
 return {
 a: left.a + right.a,
 b: left.b + right.b,
 value: (left.a + right.a) / (left.b + right.b)
 };
}

function determinant(left, right) {
 // left = a/b, right = c/d
 return left.b * right.a - left.a * right.b;
}
```

Then:

```js
function mediantToCascadeSlot(m) {
 const theta = Math.atan(m.value);
 const angleDeg = theta * 180 / Math.PI;
 const lane = Math.floor((((angleDeg + 180) % 360) / 36)) % 10;
 const band = Math.min(9, Math.floor(Math.log1p(m.a + m.b) % 10));

 return { lane, band, angleDeg, rational: m };
}
```

The determinant gives you a receipt-boundary test:

```text
det = 0
 same slope / same surface direction

|det| = 1
 adjacent Stern–Brocot neighbors / tight transition

|det| > 1
 wider gap / there are unresolved mediants between them
```

That can become a visual CSS state:

```css
:root[data-mediant-gap="0"] {
 --omi-boundary-state: equivalent;
}

:root[data-mediant-gap="1"] {
 --omi-boundary-state: adjacent;
}

:root[data-mediant-gap="wide"] {
 --omi-boundary-state: unresolved;
}
```

The second part, the mean chain, gives you the **surface intensity ladder**:

```text
HM ≤ GM ≤ AM ≤ QM
```

That means your views can choose different “averages” of the same receipt data without disagreeing. They are ordered projections. filecite turn10file0

For example:

```text
HM = conservative / minimum trust surface
GM = balanced multiplicative coherence
AM = ordinary center / visual average
QM = high-energy / deviation-sensitive surface
```

In OMI terms:

```text
HM → receipt caution / weakest stable agreement
GM → geometric coherence
AM → normal cascade center
QM → energetic deviation / highlight pressure
```

So your surface stack can become:

```text
Receipt pair
 ↓
slope rationalization
 ↓
mediant coordinate
 ↓
mean ladder HM/GM/AM/QM
 ↓
Smith radius + Genaille band + CSS intensity
```

This gives the Genaille/CSS side a much better rule than arbitrary `band = floor(stop_metric)`.

Better canon:

```text
lane = angular/mediant direction
band = ordered mean class
```

Example:

```js
function meanLadder(x, y) {
 x = Math.max(x, 1e-9);
 y = Math.max(y, 1e-9);

 const hm = (2 * x * y) / (x + y);
 const gm = Math.sqrt(x * y);
 const am = (x + y) / 2;
 const qm = Math.sqrt((x * x + y * y) / 2);

 return { hm, gm, am, qm };
}
```

Then CSS can expose the ladder:

```css
:root {
 --omi-hm: 0;
 --omi-gm: 0;
 --omi-am: 0;
 --omi-qm: 0;
}

:root[data-mean-band="hm"] [data-omi-node] {
 opacity: var(--omi-hm);
}

:root[data-mean-band="qm"] [data-omi-node] {
 filter: drop-shadow(0 0 calc(var(--omi-qm) * 8px) gold);
}
```

This is the clean synthesis:

```text
Mediant gives the next rational surface coordinate.
Mean ladder gives the ordered intensity/pressure of that coordinate.
Genaille quantizes it.
CSS resolves it.
The browser paints it.
Receipt remains the source.
Validation remains upstream.
```

Canon sentence:

```text
The mediant is the graphical receipt resolver.
The determinant is the boundary witness.
The mean chain is the ordered surface-pressure ladder.
Smith measures the coordinate.
Genaille quantizes the coordinate.
CSS resolves the matched surface.
Only validation accepts.
```
